advantages: Clear separation of concerns: offline training for accuracy and experimentation; online inference optimized for latency and cost.
Frozen inference is deterministic and auditable — same x and θ always yield the same ŷ, simplifying testing and compliance.
Training can use large labeled datasets and heavy compute once; inference scales horizontally across cheap serving replicas.
fit/predict APIs make the two phases explicit in code, reducing accidental training during production requests.
Evaluation on held-out labeled data before deployment catches overfitting without mixing training updates into inference paths.
disadvantages: Deployed models become stale as data drifts — inference quality degrades until a new training cycle refreshes θ.
Training–serving skew: if feature pipelines differ between fit() and predict() environments, inference silently misbehaves.
Inference infrastructure must handle peak traffic, versioning, and rollback — operational complexity beyond the training job itself.
Cannot adapt to individual requests at inference time without additional online learning machinery (not standard fit/predict).
Large models may be too slow or memory-heavy for real-time inference even when training succeeded on powerful GPUs.
